title: EARA
plain_summary: This law helps people get quicker decisions when they disagree with the Department of the Interior about how land can be used. When someone appeals a decision to the Interior Board of Land Appeals, they can ask for their case to be handled faster. If they make this request, the board must make a final decision within six months. If the board does not meet this deadline, the person can take their case straight to court without waiting any longer.
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources Subcommittee on Public Lands, Forests, and Mining. Hearings held.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Expedited Appeals Review Act or EARA for short.

sections.title: Section 2  Expedited Reviews
sections.text: When someone disagrees with a decision made by the Department of the Interior about land use, they can appeal that decision to a group called the Board of Land Appeals. If someone wants their appeal to be handled faster, they can write a letter asking for expedited review (which means speeded up review). If they ask for this faster review, the Board of Land Appeals must make a final decision within six months of getting that letter. However, the Board gets at least eighteen months from when the appeal was first filed before this six month deadline starts. If the Board does not make a final decision by the required deadline, then the original Department of the Interior decision becomes final for legal purposes. When this happens, if someone wants to challenge the decision in court, the court will review the case completely from the beginning instead of just checking if the agency made any obvious mistakes. This new process applies to appeals that are already waiting to be decided when this law takes effect, and to any new appeals filed after this law starts. If there is ever a conflict between this new six month deadline and deadlines in other laws about oil and gas payments or surface mining, this new six month deadline is the one that must be followed.
